text:
of courts and a pre-existing order of a Foreign Court must yield to 
the best interests of the child, especially when the Court has decided 
to conduct an elaborate enquiry in this regard.7 Such cases must be 
decided on the sole and predominant criterion of ‘what would serve 
the interests and welfare’ of the minor.8 The pre-existing order of a 
Foreign Court is merely one of the circumstances to consider when 
assessing the best interests and welfare of the person concerned.9 
This doctrine was evolved to protect children who may, unwittingly,
